steps.title: Identify the structure needed
steps.content: The sentence has a complete main clause ending with snow and ice cover. What follows must attach to that clause and express its result — the monkeys hunting for marine animals. A nonfinite participial phrase is the correct structure to show this consequence.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice by rule
steps.content:
Rule: a finite verb cannot be added after a complete clause without a coordinating conjunction; a nonfinite participle can follow a main clause to express result or consequence.
  • Choice A — 'forces': finite present-tense verb — creates a run-on sentence by adding a second main verb without a conjunction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'to force': infinitive — produces a dangling infinitive with no clear logical subject attached to the clause. ✗
  • Choice C — 'forcing': present participle — correctly forms a participial phrase after the main clause, showing that the lack of food sources results in the monkeys hunting. ✓
  • Choice D — 'forced': past participle used alone — leaves an incomplete, ambiguous participial structure with no grammatical anchor. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
